coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence enhancement via varied long-chain thiol stabilized gold nanoparticles: A study of far-field effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Jana, Jayasmita
Aditya, Teresa
Ganguly, Mainak
Mehetor, Shyamal Kumar
Pal, Tarasankar
coredata.dc:description: Metal enhanced fluorescence of carbon dots has been reported in aqueous solution. Moderately fluorescing carbon dots (λex =360nm and λem =440nm) of 6–8nm diameters (CDA) have been synthesized from freshly prepared aqueous ascorbic acid solution under modified hydrothermal treatment. The CDA fluorescence is quenched at the close proximity with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). Here, a substrate specific near-field electric field distribution is pronounced. Anticipating distance dependent fluorescence enhancement phenomenon, long-chain aliphatic thiol capped AuNPs are introduced to improve fluorescence of moderately fluorescing CDAs. The long-chain aliphatic thiols act as spacers between CDA and AuNP. Interestingly, the fluorescence of CDA is observed to be enhanced successively as the chain lengths of aliphatic thiols are increased. Fluorescing CDA, upon excitation, transfers energy to the nearby AuNP and a plasmon is induced. This plasmon radiates in the far-field resulting in fluorescence enhancement of CDAs. Such an interesting enhancement in emission with metallic gold is termed as gold enhanced fluorescence. This far-field effect for fluorescence enhancement of CDA particles becomes a general consensus in solution with varied long-chain aliphatic amine ligand capped silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). Finally, consequence of far-field effect of fluorescence enhancement has been observed while derivatized AuNP and AgNP are introduced into the CDA solution simultaneously which is described as reinforced fluorescence enhancement due to coupled plasmonic radiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold enhanced fluorescence
Carbon dots
Thiols
Chain length

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Gold Nanoparticles and Thiol Conjugation
Effect of Gold Nanoparticles on the Fluorescence of Carbon Dots
Use of Aliphatic Thiols as Spacers Between Gold Nanoparticles and Carbon Dots to Attain Gold Enhanced Fluorescence
Effect of Morphologically Different Gold Nanoparticles on the Fluorescence of Carbon Dots
Effect of Aliphatic Thiols on the Fluorescence of Carbon Dots
Application of GEF for Sulfide ion Sensing
Silver Enhanced Fluorescence Using Aliphatic Amines as Spacers
Collective and Comprehensive Effect of Thiol Capped Gold Nanoparticles and Amine Capped Silver Nanoparticles on the Fluorescence of Carbon Dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and (B) spectral profile of the as-synthesized carbon dots, CDA.
TEM image of as-synthesized (A) gold nanorod, NR, (B) gold quasi-sphere, NS. (C) Absorption spectra of as-synthesized NR and NS particles in aqueous solution.

Emission spectral profile of as-synthesized CDA, CDA-NR and CDA-NS in aqueous solution. Condition: [CDA]=0.001gmL−1and [NR or NS]=0.0015gmL−1 and final volume=3mL solution. (λex =360nm and λem =440nm).

Fluorescence spectral profile of as-synthesized (A) thiol capped NR-CDA and (B) thiol capped NS-CDA in aqueous solution (λex =360nm and λem =440nm). (C) Absorption spectra of CDA, NR, NS, thiol capped NR-CDA and thiol capped NS-CDA solution, thiol=C18SH. Condition: [CDA]=0.001gmL−1and [NR or NS]=0.0015gmL−1, [thiol]=8×10−4 M and final volume=3mL.

The plot shows the relative fluorescence intensity vs. chain length of thiol (CnSH) as the effect of addition of different thiol into aqueous solution of CDA, CDA-NR and CDA-NS. Condition: [CDA]=0.001gmL−1, [NR or NS]=0.0015gmL−1 L, [thiol]=8×10−4 M and final volume=3mL. (λex =360nm and λem =440nm).

TEM image of (A) GNP1, (B) GNP2, (C) GNP3 and (D) GNP4. (E) Absorption spectra of GNP1, GNP2, GNP3 and GNP4. (F) Fluorescence spectral profile of CDA in presence of GNP1, GNP2, GNP3, GNP4, GNP1-C18SH, GNP2-C18SH, GNP3-C18SH and GNP4-C18SH. Condition: [CDA]=0.001gmL−1, [GNP]=0.0015gmL−1 0.20mL, [C18SH]=8×10−4 M and final volume=3mL. (λex =360nm and λem =440nm).
Spectral profile of CDA in the presence of different long-chain thiols (λex =360nm and λem =440nm). Condition: [CDA]=0.001gmL−1, [thiol]=8×10−4 M and final volume=3mL.
Pictorial representation of thiol capped gold mediated fluorescence enhancement of carbon dots in aqueous medium.

(A) Fluorescence spectral profile of NS-C18SH-CDA showing the effect of different anions and (B) the corresponding bar diagram (I0/I is the relative fluorescence intensity, I and I0 are fluorescence intensities of NS-C18SH-CDA with individual anion and without anion, respectively) showing comparative quenching. (C) Bar diagram showing the relative fluorescence intensity of the control and S2−-NS-C18SH-CDA in presence of different anions. (D) Spectral profile showing sulfide ion concentration dependent fluorescence of NS-C18SH-CDA solution. (E) Relative fluorescence intensity vs. [sulfide] plot and linear dynamic range (inset). Condition: [NS-C18SH-CDA]=0.0034gmL−1, [Anions]=0.1mL 10−2 M and total volume=3mL (λex =360nm and λem =440nm).

Fluorescence spectral profile of (A) CDA, AgNP-CDA and amine conjugated AgNP-CDA and (B) CDA in the presence of NR-C18SH, NS-C18SH, AgNP-C16NH, mixture of NR-C18SH AgNP-C16NH and mixture of NS-C18SH AgNP-C16NH Condition: [CDA]=0.001gmL−1 and [AgNP]=0.0015gmL−1, [NR or NS]=0.0015gmL−1, [amine/C18SH]=8×10−4 M and final volume=3mL (λex =360nm and λem =440nm).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence enhancement via varied long-chain thiol stabilized gold nanoparticles: A study of far-field effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Furman University, Greenville, SC 29613, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Kolkata 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal enhanced fluorescence of carbon dots has been reported in aqueous solution. Moderately fluorescing carbon dots (λex =360nm and λem =440nm) of 6–8nm diameters (CDA) have been synthesized from freshly prepared aqueous ascorbic acid solution under modified hydrothermal treatment. The CDA fluorescence is quenched at the close proximity with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). Here, a substrate specific near-field electric field distribution is pronounced. Anticipating distance dependent fluorescence enhancement phenomenon, long-chain aliphatic thiol capped AuNPs are introduced to improve fluorescence of moderately fluorescing CDAs. The long-chain aliphatic thiols act as spacers between CDA and AuNP. Interestingly, the fluorescence of CDA is observed to be enhanced successively as the chain lengths of aliphatic thiols are increased. Fluorescing CDA, upon excitation, transfers energy to the nearby AuNP and a plasmon is induced. This plasmon radiates in the far-field resulting in fluorescence enhancement of CDAs. Such an interesting enhancement in emission with metallic gold is termed as gold enhanced fluorescence. This far-field effect for fluorescence enhancement of CDA particles becomes a general consensus in solution with varied long-chain aliphatic amine ligand capped silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). Finally, consequence of far-field effect of fluorescence enhancement has been observed while derivatized AuNP and AgNP are introduced into the CDA solution simultaneously which is described as reinforced fluorescence enhancement due to coupled plasmonic radiation.

Gold nanoparticle mediated enhancement of fluorescence of carbon dots in the solution phase.
Image 2

ce:section.ce:para: In fluorescence spectroscopy the fluorescence of a bulk material is measured in solution where the solutions are typically transparent to emitted radiation and isotropic emission of the fluorophore in the free space. This is observed in the far-field due to change in the medium refractive index. The free space spectral properties of a fluorophore are very minutely influenced with the change in refractive index of the medium. However, in the presence of certain metal surface, there happens a huge change in the fluorescence of the fluorophore due to modifications in free space spectral behaviour [1]. Metal nanoparticle (MNP) has the ability to manipulate light and hence light–matter interaction is affected at the nanoregion. The radiative decay rate of emitters placed in the near-field of MNPs can easily be influenced owing to the existence of localized plasmon plasmons of MNPs [2]. Mohammadi et al. [3] studied nanoantennas of spheroidal MNPs as a function of aspect ratio, volume, background index, and metal. MNP like gold (Au), silver (Ag), and copper (Cu) with loosely bound surface electrons can easily show up surface plasmon resonance in the visible range, and are chosen as good nanoantennas. In the presence of these MNPs some fluorophores show enhanced fluorescence or metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) [2]. The MEF phenomenon follows two pathways: enhancement of the local electric field due to localized surface plasmon resonance and coupling between surface plasmon field of metal and the molecular dipole of fluorophore [1]. Lakowicz explained the MEF phenomenon with the help of radiating plasmon (RP) model [4]. According to the RP model, enhancement and quenching of fluorescence of a fluorophore near the metal surface are determined by the optical properties of the metal structures calculated from electrodynamics. The extent of the enhancement also relates to the geometry of the metallic nanostructures contributing different surface plasmonic modes at “hotspots” [5].

MNPs show salient optical behaviour as they possess surface plasmons that can be excited by incident light [6]. This results in a significant enhancement of the electromagnetic field at the MNP surface. The surface plasmon of MNP directly depends on the particle shape, size and many other factors. Hence, just by modifying those factors this enhanced near field can be used to design highly sensitive chemical and biosensors with specific plasmon resonances. MNPs are found to be efficient to tune emissive behaviour of a fluorophore depending on the distance of the fluorophore from MNP surface. The fluorescence enhancement can be attributed to a combination of processes including enhanced absorption by the molecule, modification of the radiative decay rate of the molecule, and enhanced coupling efficiency of the fluorescent emission towards the far-field. When MNP is excited in presence of incident light, charges would be concentrated at the metal-dielectric interface resulting in a strong amplification of local electric field. This phenomenon is known as ‘lightening rod effect’ that increases the rate of fluorophore emission in the vicinity of MNPs [2]. MEF has innumerable applications in sensing [7,8], drug delivery [9], biotechnology [10] etc.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a potential fluorescent probe for various applications [11] including chemical sensing [12], bioimaging [13], catalysis [14], fluorescent markers [15], reductant [16] etc. Scientists have described CDs as highly fluorescent surface-passivated small carbon nanoparticles [17] with sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons atoms with plenty of oxygen-containing groups [18]. In general, CDs are nontoxic, cytocompatible, water soluble and photostable [19]. Tiny size of CDs causes quantum confinement of emissive energy traps on particle surface [20] which causes intense fluorescence. Some other reasons behind the fluorescence of CDs is radiative recombination of excitons, HOMO-LUMO energy gap, triplet carbene at zigzag ends, surface passivation etc. [18]. The emissive property can also be further improved by doping of hetero atoms like boron, nitrogen, phosphorous etc. [21]. MEF has been proved to be another way of looking at the enhancement on fluorescence of CDs [22]. In the present study gold enhanced fluorescence of CDs has been described where quantum yield is observed to be enhanced.

Various studies have shown that when fluorophore is directly attached to metal surface or located at a very short distance [23], its fluorescence is quenched. However, at a reasonably moderate distance, the presence of metal surface plasmon modifies the radiative decay rate of fluorophore [23,24]. That is actually observed in the present case even with metal particles of altered shapes. The shape of nanoparticle is an important factor especially in the study of plasmonics. So we have synthesized two types of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs); rods (NRs) and quasi-sphere (NSs). NRs and NSs find application in medical science. Previously Gandra et al. [25] utilized gold NRs for fluorescence enhancement of organic dye employing their tunable surface plasmon resonance phenomenon over a broad range. Upon addition of AuNP (both NR and NS individually), the fluorescence of CDA is effectively quenched. The quenching effect steeply decreases near the surface of MNP than what is observed from enhanced local electromagnetic field. Hence a specific separation from MNP surface to fluorophore should show up an enhancement in fluorescence of fluorophore. In many instances core-shell structure has been implemented to maintain such distance [23,26]. In some cases, salen ligands [2], synthetic organic molecules and polymers [27], polyelectrolyte multilayers [28] etc. are used as spacers to maintain suitable distance between MNPs and fluorophore. In our study we have demonstrated successful enhancement of weak fluorescence of CDs employing variable long-chain aliphatic thiol spacers in between AuNPs and CDs in solution phase.

Throughout the experiment AR grade reagents and triple distilled water were used. Ascorbic acid (AA), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4.3H2O), cetyl trimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), trisodium citrate dehydrate, sodium borohydride, silver nitrate (AgNO3), ethanol, hydrochloric acid (HCl), 1-octanethiol, 1-decanethiol, 1-dodecanethiol, 1-tetradecanethiol, 1-hexadecanethiol, 1-octadecanethiol, hexadecylamine, tetradecylamine, dodecylamine, decylamine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All the reagents were used in the experiment without further purification. All glassware were cleaned with freshly prepared aqua regia, rinsed with sufficient amount of distilled water, and dried well before use.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on Evolution 201 spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific). The fluorescence measurements were performed at room temperature using a LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). TEM analyses were carried out with JEOL JEM-1400 plus electron microscope, using an electron source of 120kV. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed with BRUKER-AXS-D8-ADVANCE diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5418Å) in the 2θ range of 10°–90° at a scanning rate of 0.5min−1. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) studies were carried out with a Thermo-Nicolet continuum FTIR microscope. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured with Easy life V (Optical Building Blocks Corporation) equipped with a 380nm LED excitation source. A nonlinear least squares (χ2) fit was tested to determine the fit of the decay rate to a sum of exponentials and a visual inspection of the residuals and the autocorrelation function were used to determine the quality of the fit. The samples were taken in a quartz cuvette of 1cm path length for fluorescence measurement. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) studies were carried out using a Malvern Nano ZS instrument equipped with a thermostated sample chamber.

Carbon dot (CDA) was obtained from freshly prepared ascorbic acid (AA) solution. In the typical synthesis 6mL of 10−2 M freshly prepared AA was poured into a 15mL borosil screw capped test tube. Then the screw cap test tube was kept in the upright position in front of 200W bulb (Philips India) at a distance of 3cm for 7h. The whole system was fitted in a 1ft.×1ft.×1ft. closed wooden box. This method of heating a reaction mixture is termed as modified hydrothermal (MHT) reaction [29]. After 7h, a pale yellow coloured solution was obtained that showed fluorescence emission at 440nm when excited at 360nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Gold Nanoparticles and Thiol Conjugation
ce:section.ce:para: Two types of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were synthesized to use them to study gold enhanced fluorescence studies. One is quasi-sphere (NS) and the other is rod (NR). Both the AuNPs were synthesized according to a method proposed by Sau et al. [30]. For NS particles, initially 7.5mL 0.1M CTAB solution was mixed with 0.25mL 10−2 M HAuCl4·3H2O solution in a 15mL vial. It results in a yellow coloured solution. After 1min, 0.6mL 10−2 M ice cold sodium borohydride solution was added to the yellow solution. The solution colour changed to brown. This brown coloured solution was kept on a water bath for 1h and after that the solution was kept at room temperature. After one day of aging, this solution colour turned reddish brown. This solution was then centrifuged and the colourless supernatant was discarded. The solid residue was washed with water to remove excess CTAB [31]. Finally the solid was dissolved in deionised water. Then the as obtained solution was used for our experiment. For NR particles the initial stage was repeated up to heating in water bath. After 1h the vial was taken out from water bath and kept at room temperature for another 1h. Then 0.01mL of this solution was added to a mixture of 4.5mL 0.1M CTAB, 0.20mL 10−2 M HAuCl4.3H2O, 0.30mL 10−2 M AgNO3 and 0.032mL 0.1M ascorbic acid. The resulting solution was colourless. Then the solution was kept undisturbed for 1day for aging. After one day the solution colour turned reddish purple. This solution was then centrifuged and the colourless supernatant was discarded. The solid residue was washed with water to remove excess CTAB [31]. Finally the solid was dissolved into deionised water. The as obtained solution was used for further experiment.

For synthesis of thiol capped AuNPs, 0.015g vacuum dried NR/NS was dissolved in 1mL distilled water followed by 2mL 10−2 M thiol (prepared in ethanol) solutions individually and kept for one day before use.

For the preparation of CDA-AuNP solution, 0.001gmL−1 as-synthesized CDA was mixed with 0.0015gmL−1 AuNP solution taken in 3mL aqueous medium (NR or NS individually). Then this mixture was aged for 12h. For the preparation of thilolated AuNP-CDA solution, 0.001gmL−1 as-synthesized CDA solution was mixed with 0.3mL thiol capped AuNP solution (NR or NS individually) and the total solution was diluted to 3mL with distilled water and then this mixture was aged for 12h. All experiments were performed after the elapse of 12h time.

The quantum yield (φ) of CDAs was measured according to a reference point method [32]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1m H2SO4 (literature quantum yield 0.54) was used as the standard reference. The quantum yield of a sample (sl) was measured with respect to the known quantum yield of the standard reference (st). The following formula was used for the calculation,
φ sl
=
φ st
I sl
/
I st
η 2
sl
/
η 2
st
A st
/
A sl
where I is the fluorescence intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript “st” refers to standard reference with known φ and “sl” for the sample.

Carbon dots (CDA, quantum yield=2.03%) are synthesized from modified hydrothermal treatment of freshly prepared aqueous ascorbic acid (AA) solution. The as-synthesized particles show fluorescence at 440nm when excited at 360nm (Fig. 1 ). The precursor, AA itself is nonfluorescent. However on aging it degrades but does not show fluorescence. Photoluminescence of carbon dots is attributed to the surface energy traps present at the surface of the carbon dots. These emissive sites are affected by the presence of external agents [23] resulting in modification of the fluorescence property of carbon dots. In absorption spectra of CDA, a prominent peak is found at 288nm for π-π* transition. Careful TEM analysis shows that the CDA particle diameters lie in the range of 6–8nm. In XRD, a broad peak is found around 23.5o corresponding to (220) plane of carbon (JCPDS file no. 85-0409). In XPS spectra, peaks are found at 284.02eV and 532.3eV for C1s and O1s, respectively. Further analysis indicates the presence of CC, CC and CO bonds (Fig. S1). FTIR study shows the presence of the CO, CC bonds. In recent times, carbon dots have emerged as a new fluorophore for different applications in fluorescence and microscopy. In this work we have demonstrated that plasmonic MNPs in association with as-synthesized carbon dots exhibit enhanced fluorescence like classical fluorophores.

In this experiment, firstly, two different shaped gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are synthesized, nanorod (NR) and nanoquasi-sphere (NS) (Fig. 2 ). TEM image shows that NS particles are of ~23nm diameter and NR particles are ~30nm long and ~9nm wide (aspect ratio=~3.33). NR particles show two absorption peaks one at 550nm and the other at 750nm for transverse and longitudinal vibrations, respectively and the NS particles show absorption at 530nm. XRD peaks for NR at 38.4°, 44.6°, 64.7°, 77.5°, 82.3° indicates presence of (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) planes of Au(0), respectively and for NS at 38.4°, 44.5°, 64.8°, 77.5°, 82.4° indicates presence of (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) planes of Au(0), respectively according to JCPDS no. 04-0784 [33] (Fig. S2). The as-synthesized AuNPs do not possess emissive property as such.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Gold Nanoparticles on the Fluorescence of Carbon Dots
ce:section.ce:para:
When CDA solution is mixed with aqueous NS and NR solutions individually, the fluorescence of CDA is quenched (Fig. 3 ). Such quenching is attributed to substrate specific changes in the near-field electric field distributions [34]. In general, close proximity of fluorophore and metal causes induction of plasmon to metal surface. At close distance, the induced electron oscillations in the metal become more closely spaced. However, without proper execution of optical conditions, the generated plasmon will be trapped and lossy surface waves will be generated causing heat dissipation. This results in quenching of fluorescence. When metal surface is too close to the CDA surface, the plasmonic dipole may hinder the radiative recombination process within CDA. This may cause quenching in fluorescence of CDA as radiative recombination is an important factor behind the origin of fluorescence of carbon dots [35]. Whenever light is incident on NPs, both absorption and scattering occur. The absorption and scattering efficiencies of a NP is related by the equation
Q abs
=
Q ext
−
Q sca

Q abs, Q ext and Q sca are absorption, extinction and scattering efficiencies. However for particles with dimension ~20nm, total extinction is controlled by absorption [36]. The as-synthesized particles are ~20nm, hence the extinction is mostly governed by absorption. There is no reason to consider the fact that the gold nanoparticles can reabsorb the CDA emitted photons as the emission and plasmon resonance are wide apart. For quantitative information, the emission due to CDA is observed at 440nm (λem) and plasmon resonance occurs at ~530nm for gold nanoparticles.

A general conclusion is, in the absence of any MNPs, fluorophores emit light into free space. However, in presence of fluorophore the near-field of the MNP is enhanced which results in the increase in the amount of light absorbed by the fluorophore. And there occurs a coupling between dipole of fluorophore and plasmon of MNP, this in turn increases the radiative decay rate of the fluorophore. Again radiative decay rate (Γ) is related to lifetime (τ) and quantum yield (φ) of fluorophore as:
Γ = φ / τ

τ and φ can be expressed as:
τ = 1 /
Γ +
K nr
and , φ = Γ /
Γ +
K nr
where, K nr is the nonradiative decay rate for excited state relaxation. However in the presence of MNPs, the system gets modified and the equations change accordingly including a new term, Γ m , system modified radiative rate [10].
τ = 1 /
Γ +
Γ m
+
K nr
and
φ =
Γ +
Γ m
/
Γ +
Γ m
+
K nr

The equations for lifetime of fluorophore with and without metal are different. The time resolved emission spectra were obtained for this purpose. It was found that the average lifetime of CDA, NS-CDA and NR-CDA are 5.12ns, 2.77ns and 3.45ns, respectively (Fig. S3). The decrease in lifetime of AuNP-CDA system is attributed to the nonradiative dissipation of energy within metals which includes lossy surface waves, dissipated losses, ohmic losses etc. [2]. The decrease in lifetime is associated with the decrease in quantum yield. Also this decrease in lifetime goes with quenching of fluorescence in the presence of NS and NR particles. The intensity and position of emission band of CDA change with excitation wavelength. Hence the lifetime measurements are carried out with respect to 360nm excitation wavelength at which CDAs show maximum emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Use of Aliphatic Thiols as Spacers Between Gold Nanoparticles and Carbon Dots to Attain Gold Enhanced Fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: According to radiating plasmon model, the enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of a fluorophore is not exclusively considered as the result of plasmon of the MNPs alone. Rather the association of MNP and excited fluorophore can act as a single radiating entity. However, increase in fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore in the presence of metal surface is a result of increased rate of excitation and intrinsic radiative decay rate. The rate of excitation is increased due to MNP induced concentration of local electric field on fluorophore (lightening rod effect) [1]. MNP can also enhance the photon emission rate of the fluorophore. This enhances the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore with the decrease in the lifetime. The lightening rod effect and near-field electromagnetic field enhancement are categorised under surface coupled enhanced fluorescence. However in the present case, the fluorescence enhancement is accompanied by increment in lifetime. This is probably due to modification of radiative decay rate. As metal is separated from the fluorophore by a spacer, the fluorophore interacts with metal through far-field radiation [4].

As the excitation wavelength of fluorophore CDA (360nm) and the plasmon maximum of Au(0) (520nm) are different, one may think that the enhancement in fluorescence of CDA is due to some kind of chemical interaction. From UV–Vis spectroscopy we find that the CDA-thiol capped Au(0) system shows peaks of Au(0) and CDA. As peak positions of Au(0) and CDA are not changed or no new peak is generated, only physical interaction between CDA and thiol capped Au(0) is considered instead of chemical interaction (Fig. 4C). Furthermore, CDA and CDA-thiol capped Au(0) emit at the same position i.e. at 440nm. Swiping of excitation wavelength for both the systems shows that 360nm is the suitable excitation wavelength that shows maximum fluorescence intensity. Moreover, 360nm is the excitation of the fluorophore CDA. After excitation, fluorophore may transfer energy to gold nanoparticles. Then, the excited Au(0) particles emit radiating plasmons, causing fluorescence enhancement. In this context, Ganguly et al. [1,2] also demonstrated that for obtaining coinage metal enhanced fluorescence, fluorophore should not necessarily be excited at the plasmon band of coinage metal particles.

In this experiment we have used two differently shaped AuNPs, NS and NR, with different scattering cross-sections to see whether they behave in the similar way. Both of them cause enhancement in fluorescence when they are thiol capped (Fig. 5 ). Lifetime measurements have been done for the systems that show maximum fluorescence (We have termed the systems as NR-C18SH-CDA and NS-C18SH-CDA for NR and NS, respectively). The average lifetime of NS-C18SH–CDA system and NR-C18SH-CDA systems are found to be 9.35ns and 8.34ns, respectively (Fig. S7, Table S1). The quantum yield increases to 4.1% and 3.87% for NS-C18SH-CDA and NR-C18SH-CDA system, respectively. The observation indicates the application of far-field condition in contrast to the near-field condition where system quantum yields and lifetimes change in opposition. We have tried to measure the size of the C18SH attached to the AuNP. TEM could not provide any satisfactory result hence we used DLS technique to obtain the hydrodynamic size of C18SH, attached to the AuNP surface. From DLS studies we have found that the approximate hydrodynamic size of the C18SH capped NR particle is 820nm and C18SH capped NS particle is 772nm whereas for uncapped NR and NS particles the hydrodynamic sizes are 189nm and 141nm, respectively. Then the hydrodynamic size of C18SH has been calculated by subtraction of the size of C18SH capped AuNP and diameter of uncapped AuNP. In both cases the value has been found to be ~630nm. Hence, we conclude that the hydrodynamic size of C18SH is ~630nm (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Morphologically Different Gold Nanoparticles on the Fluorescence of Carbon Dots
ce:section.ce:para: Additional proof of the observed GEF for as-synthesized CDA particles is presented from a separate piece of investigation. Here we have brought four types of AuNPs (GNP1, GNP2, GNP3 and GNP4) prepared from a well-known HAuCl4 reduction and stabilization strategy (Supplementary Information). Conventional stabilizing agent citrate and reducing agent borohydride and/or ascorbic acid have been found to be effective for size control Au(0) particle syntheses. In all the cases equal volume of 2.5×10−4 M chloroauric acid was used. Only for aggregated particles preformed ~5nm Au(0) particles were used for autocatalytic growth of gold particle assembly.

TEM images and related absorbance spectra of the four types of AuNPs are shown in Fig. 6 . C18SH capped GNP1, GNP2, GNP3 and GNP4 show different enhancement for CDA upon excitation at 360nm. It is evident from the fluorescence spectra that the fluorescence intensity increases successively with the regular increase in particle size. The increased particle size relates to regular red shifted SPR peak. Much higher enhancement (than any spherical particles case) is observed for GNP4 case where particles are aggregated and aggregated particles are obtained by deliberate use of preformed Au(0) particles. The reason behind such significant enhancement is the aggregation induced fluorescence due to the high scattering cross-section of the assembly. Thus they do not involve trapping of emission from fluorophore and deject any underlying absorption [38]. Predominant aggregation of GNP4 contributes maximum enhancement effect to the observed phenomenon whereas spiky nature has negligible effect on the plasmonic behaviour of the particles. Again one may assume that all such enhancement in presence of thiol capped AuNPs is a consequence of aggregation of Au(0)NPs in the presence of thiol. It is worth mentioning that fluorescence is inherited by small (<2nm) particles i.e., clusters and is not originated from large particles [40]. It important to mention that both NR and GNP4 particles have stronger absorption at 530nm in comparison to the absorption profile at 750 and 690nm, respectively. So one may think that the particles are non-homogeneous and there are more spherical particles in the system. However this supposition is not true as we see stronger peak at 530nm even for both nonaggregated NR and aggregated GNP4 particles and TEM images discard the possibility of spherical particles. So the NR and GNP4 particles themselves show such absorbance due to transverse vibration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Aliphatic Thiols on the Fluorescence of Carbon Dots
ce:section.ce:para: A control experiment has also been performed with thiol functionalized CDA solutions and that is devoid of AuNPs. The as-synthesized CDA solution was incubated in long-chain thiol solutions [C n SH; n =8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, respectively] individually. After one day of aging the chain length dependent increment of fluorescence is observed (Fig. 7 ). When a thiol compound is added to CDA, there occurs a physical interaction between thiol (−SH) group and surface functional group of CDA, hence the aliphatic groups are exposed to solution. This imparts hydrophobic character to the CDA-thiol system [41] However this chain length dependent enhancement in fluorescence intensity is not comparable with the enhancement in presence of thiol capped AuNPs (Fig. 5). The increment in fluorescence in the presence of AuNPs can be attributed to the modification of local electromagnetic field intensity in the vicinity of CDA by the localized surface plasmon resonance at the surface of AuNPs. Thus we see that CDs show enhanced fluorescence in the presence of thiol capped AuNPs irrespective of shape and morphology.

Enhancement of emission of CDs in presence of AuNPs in aqueous medium is really new information and an interesting phenomenon. Upon addition of Au(III) salt to CDA solution, Au(III) is instantly reduced to Au(0) and then stabilized by CDA. In this process CDA loses its inherent fluorescence. The fact that thiol capped AuNP enhances the fluorescence of CDA where as in situ synthesized AuNP quenches the fluorescence can be accounted as follows. On addition of CDA solution to Au(III) Au(0) is generated and CDA works as both reducing and stabilizing agent. As both plasmonic metal and fluorophore is present in the system, there occurs an interaction between metal dielectric and fluorophore. However, we find the quenching of fluorescence. This quenching may be attributed to the following reasons. When a fluorophore is excited it induces plasmon in the nearby metal. But depending on the optical conditions, this plasmon can get trapped and there will be no radiation. Wave vector is another factor on which the radiation of plasmon is dependent [4]. Thus lossy surface waves are induced that cause quenching at very short distance between fluorophore and metal surface. Then heat is dissipated [1,4]. This also occurs when thiol free AuNP is added to the CDA solution. Another factor is that the induced plasmon may hinder the radiative recombination of CDA. As radiative recombination of excitons is another main reason behind the origin of fluorescence of CDs, hindrance in radiative recombination will cause quenching in fluorescence intensity [35].

However, if the optical conditions are changed and local geometry permits, lossy surface waves can be recovered and plasmonic radiation can occur at far-field [4]. Here comes the importance of aliphatic thiol capped AuNPs. Aliphatic spacers can attain different rotameric arrangement that prevent the close approach of the CDA towards the Au(0) surface [2]. Then the excited fluorophore transfers energy to AuNP. The excited Au(0) particles emit radiating plasmons, causing fluorescence enhancement of the fluorophore, CDA.
So we have found another way to tune the fluorescence of CDs using AuNPs, schematically represented in Fig. 8 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of GEF for Sulfide ion Sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The importance of long-chain aliphatic thiols as spacers is further confirmed when sulfide ion is added to the thiol spacer mediated fluorescent systems. It is found that upon addition of sulfide to the NS/NR-C18SH-CDA solution, the fluorescence of the system is quenched. When sulfide ion is added, it readily gets attached to the Au(0) surface as Au(0) has inherent affinity towards sulfide ion. Sulfide ion is quite smaller in size than long-chain thiol, so it can easily diffuse to Au(0) surface and replace the thiol chain. Thus the distance between Au(0) surface and CDA surface decrease and the plasmonic coupling hinders the radiative recombination [35] of CDA system causing quenched fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Silver Enhanced Fluorescence Using Aliphatic Amines as Spacers
Collective and Comprehensive Effect of Thiol Capped Gold Nanoparticles and Amine Capped Silver Nanoparticles on the Fluorescence of Carbon Dots
ce:section.ce:para: Already we have described the fluorescence enhancement of CDAs in presence of suitably derivatized individual AuNPs and AgNPs exploiting their plasmonic property. Now we have an interesting observation that indicates a collective and comprehensive effect while both AuNPs and AgNPs are introduced into CDA solution where the observed enhancement becomes more pronounced than the effect of individual MNP. Again, in the experiment, NR-C18SH/NS-C18SH and AgNP-C16NH are purposely introduced to observe the change. The observation is the reinforced plasmonic effect of two plasmonic nanoparticles which causes further enhancement of fluorescence. This is an effect of coupled plasmonic radiation. The enhancements are ~9 times higher than the fluorescence intensity of the as-synthesized carbon dots. This is greater than individual effect of thiol capped AuNPs (NR-C18SH/NS-C18SH) where observed enhancement is six and seven times, respectively and for AgNP-C16NH it is five times. Fig. 10 shows the respective enhancements. It is worth mentioning that the enhancement is not synergistic as the resultant effect is not more than the sum of their individual effect. It has been reported that synergism is mainly exhibited by bimetallic nanoparticles, alloy nanostructure doped nanoparticles, core-shell giant clusters, and aggregated nanoparticles [43]. In all the cases there lies a definite geometry (e.g. Au144-x Ag x (SR)60 where, SR is the stabilizing thiol group containing ligand and x is the number of atoms in cluster) and electronic structure. However in the present case the Au(0) and Ag(0) particles, without the above mentioned geometry, are added separately. So the direct interaction with definite electronic requirements between Au(0) and Ag(0) is unlikely. Further work is warranted for in depth understanding of this mechanism.

An interesting phenomenon of fluorescence enhancement of carbon dots in the presence of thiol capped AuNPs in aqueous medium has been reported. The enhancement is a result of far-field radiation. Aliphatic thiol compounds with varied chain lengths have been used as spacers between AuNP and carbon dot. The chain length of thiols have been judiciously exploited to tune the fluorescence of carbon dots in the presence of thiol capped AuNPs. As surface plasmon strongly depends on particle shape, experiments with the modified shapes of MNPs for fluorescence enhancement have also been looked into. Effect of two different plasmonic particles for reinforced fluorescence enhancement of CDs has also been documented. Such fluorescence enhancement under the experimental conditions may provide promising information to design AuNP based sensor. The report also generalises the MEF considering both gold and silver nanoparticles to further develop the excellent proposition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the UGC, DST, NST, and CSIR (Ref. No. 17-06/2012(i)EU-V), New Delhi, India, and the IIT Kharagpur for financial assistance. Authors are thankful to Prof. Nilmoni Sarkar (Department of Chemistry, IIT Kharagpur) for DLS measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: XRD spectra, XPS spectra, FT-IR spectra, fluorescence spectra, fluorescence lifetime measurement and absorption spectra. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.07.045.
